data.title: Unit Corporation: Dividend Coverage May Drop Below 1.0x In 2025
data.description:
Unit Corporation (OTCQX:UNTC) is currently paying a $1.25 per share quarterly dividend, which works out to an approximately 15% yield at its current share price.

The challenge for Unit is that declining production combined with relatively weak commodity prices may result in its dividend coverage dropping below 1.0x starting next year.

Unit does have a substantial amount of cash on hand ($81 million at the end of Q2 2024) that can be used to support its dividend. If it does that, I can see its current dividend continuing until 2028 or 2029 before it fully depletes its cash on hand.

As Unit's production declines, its G&A costs start cutting into its free cash flow more and more. Thus, it should either find ways to reduce G&A costs or sell off its businesses within the next few years.

I now estimate Unit's value (at the end of 2024) at approximately $37.50 to $39.50 per share. Unit's estimated value has been decreasing over time as its reserves get depleted (with only limited replenishment) and it pays out dividends.

2H 2024 Outlook

I'm modeling Unit's 2H 2024 results at approximately 9,250 BOEPD (18% oil) in average production. This compares to 9,714 BOEPD (19% oil) in Q2 2024 production. Unit's production has been declining due to limited investment, although there is uncertainty around production trends due to its Shattuck shut-ins. Unit's Shattuck wells were shut in during 1H 2024 due to third-party pipeline repairs.

At the current 2H 2024 strip of around $73 to $74 WTI oil and $2.30 NYMEX gas, Unit may be able to generate $39 million in oil and gas revenue during that period.

I've also assumed that Unit can average approximately 11.5 rigs in use during 2H 2024 (down from 11.9 rigs in Q2 2024), with a slightly lower average day rate than in Q2 2024. This leads to a projection of $20 million in contract drilling revenue net of costs.

Type Barrels/Mcf Per Barrel/Mcf $ Million
Oil 310,000 $71.00 $22
NGLs 441,600 $18.50 $8
Natural Gas 5,704,000 $1.60 $9
Net Contract Drilling $20
Interest Income $2
Total $61

This results in a projection that Unit will generate $25 million in free cash flow during the second half of 2024.

$ Million
Oil and Gas Operating Costs $20
Cash G&A $8
Capital Expenditures $8
Total $36

Unit's $1.25 per share quarterly dividend would add up to $24 million in 2H 2024 dividends. Unit's dividend coverage would be just over 1.0x in this scenario.

Thus, Unit's cash balance may end up at around $82 million at the end of 2024, similar to its $81 million cash balance at the end of Q2 2024.

Dividend Sustainability

Unit's longer-term challenge is that its production and reserves are likely to continue declining due to limited investment in development. Unit's upstream division participates in a small number of wells (6 gross or 0.3 net wells during 1H 2024) drilled by other operators, but this is well below what is needed to maintain production levels.

For 2025 the current strip is around $68 WTI oil and $3.10 NYMEX gas. While natural gas prices are stronger than in 2024, the lower oil strip and declining production mean that Unit may generate around $40 million in 2025 free cash flow. This would result in 0.8x dividend coverage.

The current strip for 2026 is around $66 WTI oil (CL1:COM) and $3.55 NYMEX gas (NG1:COM), which may result in around $30 million to $35 million in free cash flow. This would be 0.6x to 0.7x dividend coverage.

Unit's cash on hand could be used to sustain its current dividend for a few more years. In the above scenario, Unit would have around $55 million in cash on hand at the end of 2026 if it kept its current dividend.

At current strip, Unit may be able to maintain its current dividend until 2028 or 2029 before it runs out of cash on hand to support the dividend.

Notes On Valuation

I am currently valuing Unit at approximately $37.50 to $39.50 per share (at the end of 2024). This assumes the current strip until the end of 2025, followed by $75 WTI oil and $3.75 NYMEX gas after that.

Of this, around 46% of the value ($175 million) is attributed to Unit's drilling division, 32% ($120 million) to its upstream assets, and 22% to its cash on hand ($82 million at the end of 2024).

Unit's drilling division is projected to generate approximately $35 million in annualized revenues (net of expenses) in 2025, so the drilling division is valued at around 5x that. The upstream asset value is based on Unit's estimated reserves.

If commodity prices follow the current strip to the end of 2026 instead (and then $75 WTI oil and $3.75 NYMEX gas after that), I'd reduce Unit's estimated value by approximately $1 per share to reflect weaker near-term cash flow.

Unit's estimated value will also go down over time as it pays out dividends and its free cash flow declines (with reduced production). So in a status quo situation, Unit's estimated value at the end of 2025 would be in the low-to-mid $30s.

Unit could theoretically cover its current dividend for another four or five years with the help of cash on hand. However, I believe that Unit should probably sell its assets within a shorter timeframe unless it can reduce its G&A costs.

Unit's G&A costs are having a larger impact on its cash flow as time goes on. By 2029 (without G&A reductions), Unit's cash G&A may be around half of its cash flow before G&A.

Conclusion

Unit's free cash flow is projected to be slightly higher than its current dividend for the second half of 2024. At the current strip, Unit's dividend coverage is projected to drop to 0.8x in 2025 and 0.6x to 0.7x in 2026.

Unit does currently have $81 million in cash on hand, and that can be used to support its $1.25 per share quarterly dividend for several more years. As time goes on, though, Unit's G&A costs (unless reduced) will eat up a larger percentage of its free cash flow. Thus, Unit should consider fully selling its businesses within the next few years if it can't reduce G&A costs significantly.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Humana: Medicare Advantage May Fail Without Regulator Intervention, Threatening Core Business
data.description:
The US health insurance market is under pressure as more people disenroll from top-rated medicare plans, harming Humana (NYSE:HUM), mainly focusing on the medicare market. After many years of rising premiums/out-of-pocket costs and growing healthcare operating costs, more people appear unwilling or reluctant to pay higher rates. The denial rate on Medicare Advantage plans rose by a staggering ~56% in 2023 compared to 2022, potentially due to the usage of AI tools to assess coverage.

Although we may expect this technology to improve Humana's operating margins by lowering its payments to providers, it seems to have primarily alienated customers to mass cancellation. Although Humana disputes the data, the CMS recently reported that only 25% of Humana members will be in plans with quality ratings above four stars in 2025, down from 94% this year, indicating an immense customer backlash. To make matters worse, many health systems are dropping Medicare Advantage plans, citing excessive prior authorization, rising denial rates, and slow payments.

HUM crashed ~15% initially as the CMS report came out, leaving the stock down ~50% YoY. HUM was among the best performers in the 2010s, rising by over 7X from 2010 to its 2022 peak as the tailwind of the Affordable Care Act and a growing number of retirees led to significant adoption of Medicare Advantage plans. To some, given the sharp rise in denial rates, payment issues, and bureaucratic issues, these are now seen as the "Medicare Advantage Scam," which I feel is apt given seniors are being encouraged to pay into a program that appears unlikely to fulfill its promises.

Of course, HUM's sharp decline is not a surprise to me. In the June article "Humana: Medicare Advantage Will Never Be As Profitable As It Was," I took a bearish outlook on HUM. Since then, HUM has been down ~26%. I believe that population aging and health status among seniors pointed toward a sustained increase in Humana's utilization rate. Further, amid higher living costs and much higher hospital operating overhead, Humana needs to increase revenues but cannot without losing customers laden by living costs.

Without immense government support or healthcare reform, the Medicare Advantage program may become untenable, at least to the extent Humana's business model requires. Now that the stock has lost half its peak value, more analysts see a valuation discount entry point. To that end, I believe it is an apt time to assess the company's profit and valuation potential under the assumption it must change its business model to avoid the continued collapse of its primary revenue stream (Medicare Advantage).

Humana's Future Requires a MA Alternative

Over recent years, Humana has phased out its commercial and non-Medicare plans, focusing more on Medicare Part D and Medicare Advantage plans. Its total premiums in Q2 were ~$28B, of which $25B came from Medicare and $22.2B specifically from Individual Medicare Advantage. Its total revenue was ~$29.5B, meaning three-quarters came from individual MA. As such, if we assume the MA program will continue to struggle, Humana is left with minimal alternative revenue streams.

Fundamentally, medical care costs (paid by both people and insurers) are rising faster than the Medicare Advantage system can keep up with. This is reflected in the sharp declines in its profit margins since 2020:

As a result, Humana is increasing denials and requirements and stopping coverage in many unprofitable areas, canceling the plans of around 560K people, or 10% of its MA membership. However, it expects some to opt for other plans. Of course, hospitals are also canceling MA acceptance due to denials. Thus, hospitals where MA may be profitable for Humana may stop taking it, while Humana may stop providing it to those where it is not possible. To me, that is a tell-tale sign that the MA program is unraveling quite quickly, with CMS's indication of 2025 being alarming.

Problematically, Humana has a limited roadmap regarding how it plans to recover from this situation. In its last investor call, its managers stated that they expect its Medicare Advantage profit margins to return to 3% by 2027, "driven by the regulatory environment." In my opinion, that is akin to planning on external factors to improve its profitability, not providing insight regarding how it intends to do so on its own accord. However, the managers noted DC policy advocacy efforts it is pursuing to show its program has better outcomes at lower costs than original Medicare.

Interestingly, neither candidate has stated a clear plan regarding Medicare and Medicare Advantage. Hence, we need not speculate on that aside from the apparent lack of political motivation to fix the staggering Medicare Advantage issue. Although my view is speculative, I believe that between DC and Humana, there is a general apathy toward fixing the trend, with hopes that the issue will naturally self-correct over time.

However, Medicare enrollment and utilization should continue to rise disproportionately due to demographics. At the same time, healthcare costs continue to grow faster than inflation, so I do not see how the issue can be solved without a significant (and likely controversial) increase in government Medicare spending. At any rate, when regulators finally make a plan to fix the MA issue, I do not think ensuring Humana keeps its 3% profit margin will be their main priority.

This leaves Humana with CenterWell, which accounts for around a fifth of its usual sales. As stated in its last investor call, the firm sees growth opportunities in CenterWell and Medicaid. That said, the company also sees higher utilization and acuity headwinds in its Medicaid segment, implying it faces pressures similar to those of Medicare.

Further, in the first six months of 2023, CenterWell had $9B in revenue, compared to $9.7B in 2024. However, its income from operations was $617M on CenterWell during Q1 and Q2 of 2023, compared to $618M in 2024. Thus, although it had growth, its profitability has decreased such that it lacks income growth in the segment. In my view, that again indicates that the profit margin issues we're seeing in Medicare Advantage are shared in all its segments.

The Bottom Line

Since I covered HUM last, we've seen more rapid signs of deterioration in the Medicare Advantage program, with signs that 2025 will see significant declines in high-quality coverage that Humana depends on for profitability. The company is expected to announce its Q3 income at the end of the month, with the consensus looking for $3.39 EPS. I believe that is reasonable, as deviation in its income would likely be attributable to changes in its operating costs. In my view, there is some potential that Humana's efforts to reduce expenses using AI tools may lower its costs proportional to its current sales. However, the historical error rate of its AI tool and the issues relating to denials may upend that effort.

Still, looking into 2025 and 2026, I expect further EPS deterioration. Interestingly, despite the recent CMS data, the analyst consensus EPS outlook for the next two years continues to point toward a slight margin recovery. That said, the CMS data release did rapidly cut its two-year EPS outlook. See below:

Given the continued acceleration in Medicare Advantage issues, from higher utilization to higher denials, disenrollment, and healthcare systems and Humana mutually cutting ties, there are no clear signs that Humana's profit margins will recover as expected. Indeed, I hope this issue will worsen until government intervention becomes necessary.

In my view, although a 16.5X forward "P/E" on HUM is not exceptionally high, it is high, given the company's deteriorating profitability and growth prospects. HUM's current valuation still reflects latent expectations that Humana will return to its previous status as a healthcare growth stock with stable margins. I believe a forward "P/E" below 10X is more suitable for a company with its core business model being threatened. Ultimately, Humana's value comes from its relationships with people, healthcare systems, and Washington politicians and regulators, all of which have seemingly soured over the past two years.

Although my outlook for HUM is mildly bearish, I would not bet against it because it is exposed to immense volatility depending on regulator actions. Ultimately, Humana is a middleman between government programs, people, and healthcare systems. I expect that the next president will have to grapple with the acceleration in the Medicare system's issues, and given their lack of statements, I have no expectations regarding what either will do. HUM may rise quickly if a plan is made to fix Medicare, but I anticipate it will decline if the current course (of seemingly avoiding the issue) continues. Thus, I am only convinced that HUM will see abnormally high volatility over the coming months and years, with more sharp moves like that seen earlier this month.

data.title: AT&T: Income Appeal Is No Longer Attractive
data.description:
AT&T (NYSE:T) is offering a lower dividend yield than its closest peer Verizon and its valuation is not cheap right now, thus AT&T’s income appeal is much lower than it was in the recent past.

As I’ve covered in previous articles, historically AT&T has been a good income pick due to its high-dividend yield and attractive valuation, trading at a relatively low earnings multiple compared to peers and its own historical average. Since my last article on AT&T, almost one year ago, its shares are up by more than 45% (including dividends), beating the market by a good margin during the same time frame.

This performance has been quite good, and above my own expectations considering that AT&T has a mature business with relatively low growth prospects, showing that its shares were clearly undervalued at the time.

Taking into account this share price rally, I think it’s now a good time to analyze its most recent financial performance and investment case, to see if AT&T remains a good income play, or not, for long-term investors.

Financial Overview & Dividends

AT&T is one of the largest telecom operators in the U.S., having exposure both to fixed and mobile telecommunications across the country. Due to its large size and the industry’s high penetration in the U.S., growth prospects come mainly from market share gains over its competitors. However, as seen in the next graph, AT&T’s mobile market share has been rather stable in recent quarters at about 30%, while Verizon (VZ) has been the main loser (39.3% market share in Q4 2021, to 37.6% in Q1 2024) and T-Mobile US (TMUS) has increased its market share by about 10 basis points during this period.

This means its net customer gains in the mobile segment have been somewhat low over the past few years, even though the company has been investing in its 5G infrastructure to remain competitive in the industry, which is not a great achievement considering that wireless is an important part of its business.

Nevertheless, AT&T’s growth strategy has also been focused on investing in its fiber rollout to offer convergent offerings and gain more broadband customers, which has been successful in recent quarters. Indeed, as shown in the next graph, AT&T has been able to report stronger growth in its broadband business, supported by increasing fiber customers and average revenue per user (ARPU). Its broadband revenues increased to $2.7 billion in Q2 2024 (+7% YoY), which is much better than other business segments, and represented about 10% of total AT&T revenues in the last quarter.

However, while service revenue has been trending positively due to the company’s efforts to improve its commercial positioning, equipment sales have been weaker due to a tougher economic backdrop as high inflation has led to a rising cost of living, putting pressure on discretionary spending by consumers. Given this background, it’s not surprising that equipment sales declined by 5.4% YoY in Q2, to slightly below $4.8 billion in the quarter.

This was the main reason why AT&T’s overall revenues were slightly down compared to Q2 2023, to $29.8 billion in Q2 2024. Despite soft top-line, AT&T has made a good job regarding controlling cost growth and achieve higher efficiency in some units, for instance it has gained some operating leverage in the broadband segment, namely by the transition from legacy networks to advanced broadband infrastructure, leading to expanding margins.

Despite the inflationary environment and pressure on wage growth and other administrative costs, its operating expenses amounted to $24 billion in Q2, representing an increase of 2.2% YoY, which is an acceptable outcome considering the challenging operating environment and general cost pressures.

Due to the combination of slightly lower revenues and higher costs, its EBITDA declined by 2.2% YoY to $10.8 billion, and its EBITDA margin declined by about 60 basis points (bps) to 36.4%.

Investors should be aware that AT&T likes to highlight its adjusted EBITDA, which was higher by about $500 million compared to reported EBITDA, while in Q2 2023 there wasn’t a material different between the two metrics. Given that this is a non-GAAP metric, I think investors should consider its reported EBITDA as the best metric to be more conservative and take into account all costs to analyze its profitability, as reported EBITDA includes all gains and losses incurred by the company, even if they aren’t recurrent in future quarters.

Moreover, over the past few quarters, adjusted EBITDA has always been higher than reported EBITDA, being another sign that investors should take adjusted figures with a grain of salt, as there are always some expenses that don’t repeat in future quarters, but are still expenses that should be reflected in the company’s accounts and should be considered, in my opinion, to analyze its operating performance.

Therefore, while AT&T says that it was able to achieve margin expansion in recent quarters, this is only true when looking at adjusted EBITDA, which excludes restructuring costs and intangible amortization related to DirecTV.

Regarding DirecTV, at the end of last September, AT&T announced that it has reached an agreement to sell its 70% stake in DirecTV to its partner TPG Inc., exiting from the pay-tv business. AT&T will receive about $7.6 billion in cash payments through 2029, which will be an important contribution to reduce its debt levels and help finance its investments in 5G and fiber infrastructure.

Regarding its net income, AT&T’s profit in Q2 amounted to $3.5 billion, representing a decline of about 20% YoY, due to higher depreciation & amortization, increasing interest expenses, and lower other income. Despite lower earnings, its cash flow from operations remained quite good at $9.1 billion in the quarter, and its capital expenditures amounted to $4.4 billion. Its free cash flow was about $4.6 billion in Q2 2023, up by 9.2% YoY, showing that AT&T’s capacity to generate cash remains quite good.

For the full year, the company’s guidance can be considered positive given that AT&T expects some pick-up in consumer activity due to seasonal patterns and new services and features being launched in the second half of the year, which should be important to achieve service revenue growth of about 3%, and grow its EBITDA on an adjusted basis.

Regarding its balance sheet, AT&T continues to prioritize leverage reduction and is using a great part of its free cash flow generation to strengthen its balance sheet, aiming to achieve a net debt-to-EBITDA ratio of about 2.5x by mid-2025. At the end of Q2 2024, its net debt position was nearly $127 billion and its leverage ratio was 2.87x, thus AT&T still needs to cut its indebtedness over the coming quarters to reach its leverage ratio, assuming that its EBITDA growth should be limited over the coming quarters.

During the past twelve months, the company was able to reduce its net debt by about $5 billion, mainly through retained free cash flow, a strategy that is not expected to change much in the near term. Nevertheless, its recent decision to sell its stake in DirecTV is also a supporting factor for lower debt ahead, thus AT&T seems to be on the right path to achieve its desired leverage ratio in the next six to nine months.

What this means for its dividend and potential share buybacks is that AT&T is not much likely to change its shareholder remuneration policy in the next few quarters, but it may become more aggressive regarding capital returns during the second half of 2025.

However, this doesn’t seem to be currently expected by the street, given that AT&T’s dividend is not estimated to grow much over the next few years. AT&T’s quarterly dividend has been unchanged at $0.2775 per share (or $1.11 per share annually) since its significant cutback in 2022, and is currently expected to grow by less than 1% annually, over the coming three years, to $1.15 per share by 2027.

This seems to be quite conservative and represents a dividend payout ratio of only 50% (vs. 56% in 2023), thus I think there is room for the company to become more aggressive regarding its shareholder remuneration policy and beat dividend estimates easily in the coming years.

Moreover, its current quarterly dividend outflow is about $2 billion, which is way below its free cash flow generation, thus this is another factor supporting a higher dividend in the future, especially when AT&T reaches its leverage goal of a net debt-to-EBITDA ratio around 2.5x.

Regarding its dividend yield, AT&T is currently offering a yield of around 5.1%, which is way lower than compared to when I last covered it (it was yielding close to 7%), thus its income appeal is somewhat lower, even though it remains interesting for income-oriented investors. However, this yield is lower than compared to its closest peer Verizon, which is yielding above 6% and also offers a sustainable dividend over the long term as I’ve covered in a previous article, thus AT&T’s income appeal seems to be much less attractive than it was one year ago.

Regarding its valuation, following a strong share price rally over the last year, AT&T is currently trading at close to 10x earnings, which is at a significant premium to its historical valuation of 7.1x earnings over the past five years. This multiple is also much higher than when I last covered AT&T (was trading at only 6.4x earnings at the time), thus its shares seem now to be somewhat overvalued. This multiple is also higher than compared to Verizon right now (it’s trading at 9.4x earnings, and its historical average is 9.7x), being another signal that AT&T is not properly cheap nowadays.

Conclusion

While AT&T offers a sustainable dividend yield above 5%, this is lower than compared to Verizon, and its valuation seems to be somewhat stretched following a strong performance in recent months. Moreover, its financial performance has not been much impressive in recent quarters and growth prospects aren’t great, thus AT&T’s income appeal doesn’t seem to be great and Verizon may be a better income alternative right now.

data.title: Citigroup: Inherently Undervalued With Promising Restructuring Results - Reiterate Buy
data.description:
Citigroup Is Inherently Undervalued - Offering Opportunistic Investors With The Dual Pronged Returns

We previously covered Citigroup Inc. (NYSE:C) in July 2024, discussing why we had maintained our Buy rating then, attributed to its discounted position compared to its Tangible Book Value/Share, significantly aided by the stock's higher highs and higher lows since the October 2023 bottom.

Combined with the double-beat FQ2'24 earnings call and the promising H2'24 commentary, we believed that the bank remained well positioned to achieve its medium-term target ahead.

C YTD Stock Price

Since then, C has had a painful correction during the worst of the July/ August 2024 market rotation, potentially attributed to the uncertainty arising from the moderating inflation and robust labor market - one which has triggered the Fed's aggressive pivot by 50 basis point in the September 2024 FOMC meeting.

It goes without saying, that the moderation in borrowing costs is expected to revive borrowing activities, one that we have observed in home mortgage loans as reported by the online real estate brokerage Redfin Corporation (RDFN) - with it expected to trigger higher loan originations and potentially, profits for banks, such as C.

The same has been reported by JPMorgan Chase & Co. (JPM) in the recent earnings call, with "a pickup in mortgage applications, tiny bit of pickup in refi and our multifamily lending business," as similarly observed with C, as they grow their "mortgage portfolio as the rate environment shifts, as well as growing overall loans," significantly aided by the healthy customer base with strong credit.

In addition, the Fed's rate cut is likely to trigger C's lower cost of interest-bearing deposits from the 3.70% observed in FQ3'24 (-0.01 points QoQ/ -0.29 YoY), albeit unlikely to be near the hyper-pandemic lows of ~0.2%.

If anything, the higher deposit costs have already contributed to the bank's recent bottom-line headwinds with moderating Net Interest Income [NII] at $13.36B (-0.9% QoQ/ -3.3% YoY) and Net Interest Margins [NIM] at 2.33% by the latest quarter (-0.08 points QoQ/ -0.16 YoY), compared to a year ago at $13.83B/ 2.49%, respectively.

As a result, we believe that the Fed's ongoing rate normalization is likely to be a net positive for most big banks, C included, significantly aided by the ongoing restructuring efforts and optimization of operating expenses.

Despite the underwhelming overall RoTCE at 7% in FQ3'24 (-0.2 points QoQ/ -0.7 YoY) and 7.2% YTD (-0.9 points YoY), we are already seeing notable improvements in the Services segment's bottom-lines to 26.4% (+2.6 points QoQ/ +3.3 YoY) and 24.7% (+2.3 points YoY), respectively.

Given that the Services segment is C's top/ bottom-line driver, the improvement is highly encouraging indeed, with most of the bottom-line headwinds attributed to the mixed US Personal Banking performance with RoTCE of 8.2% (+6.3 points QoQ/ -5.5 YoY).

The lower margin is naturally attributed to the USPB's ballooning Net Credit Losses [NCL] on loans to $1.86B (-3.6% QoQ/ +38.8% YoY) and higher NCL ratio to average loans at 4.4% (-0.3 points QoQ/ +1.1 YoY), along with the increased Allowance for Credit Losses on Loans [ACLL] at 8.2% (+0.1 points QoQ/ +0.4 YoY).

When comparing to C's overall ACLL across all segments at 2.7% (+0.02 points QoQ/ +0.02 YoY), it goes without saying that the ongoing Fed rate normalization is likely to bring forth moderate volatility in the USPB's performance, as the US credit card balances reached new heights of $1.14 T by Q2'24 (+2.7% QoQ/ +10.6% YoY/ +31% from Q2'19 levels).

Even so, we believe that the headwinds are temporal since inflation is already cooling with the labor market still healthy, with the "signs of stress isolated to the lower FICOs."

At the same time, we expect to see C's unrealized losses for held-to-maturity [HTM] debt securities to moderate from the $20.94B reported in FQ2'24 (-1% QoQ/ -13% YoY) and peak of $25.31B reported in FY2022, with FQ3'24 data yet available.

Our optimism arises from the slow and sure macroeconomic normalization process, with it improving optics surrounding the Big Four, C included, especially since most banks usually hold their HTM securities to maturity.

US Treasuries Yield Curve

This recovery process is significantly aided by the normalization in the US Treasury Yield curve after the worst of the post-pandemic fears and the US banking meltdown, as the market prices in a 25 basis point rate cut in the upcoming November 2024 FOMC meeting - with it triggering brighter prospects ahead.

The Consensus Forward Estimates

As a result of the robust Services top/ bottom-line expansion and the potential recovery on the USPB segment over the next few quarters of normalization, we believe that the consensus forward estimates appear to be promising indeed, with C expected to report accelerated bottom-line expansions in FY2025/ FY2026.

Much of the tailwinds are naturally attributed to the bank's ongoing restructuring efforts, with 20K headcounts expected to be made redundant by 2026 - significantly aided by the supposed Banamex IPO by the end of 2025.

C Valuations

And this is also why we believe that C remains cheap at FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 11.53x, while elevated compared to its 5Y mean of 9.14x and 10Y mean of 9.50x, with it seemingly reasonable compared to the sector median of 12.28x.

When comparing C's extremely cheap FWD PEG non-GAAP ratio of 0.44x to the sector median of 1.37x and its Big Bank peers, including JPM at 4.74x, Wells Fargo & Company (WFC) at 1.39x, and Bank of America Corporation (BAC) at 1.35x, it goes without saying that the former offers a compelling investment thesis - with it offering interested investors with an improved margin of safety.

So, Is C Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

C 5Y Stock Price

For now, C has continued to trade sideways after the immense recovery from October 2023 bottoms, with it also triggering a robust upside potential of +43.1% to its Tangible Book Value of $89.67 per share (+2.4% QoQ/ +3.1% YoY).

These numbers do not appear to be overly aggressive indeed, attributed to the promising long-term price target of $80.50, based on the consensus FY2026 adj EPS estimates of $8.47 and the 10Y P/E mean of 9.50x.

At the same time, C continues to offer a more than decent dividend investment thesis at forward yields of 3.39% compared to its 5Y mean of 2.61% and the sector median of 3.13% - with it allowing long-term shareholders to subscribe to their DRIP program and accumulate additional shares on a quarterly basis.

As a result of the promising restructuring progress and the robust capital appreciation/ dividend income prospects, we are reiterating our Buy rating for the C stock here.

data.title: U.S. Dollar Strengthens As Other Major Central Banks Cut Policy Rates
data.description:
The Federal Reserve seems to be on a different page than other major central banks.

In the United States, economic growth is strong.

In Europe and in England...that is not the case.

The European Central Bank just lowered its policy rate of interest, by 25 basis points.

The Euro dropped sharply against the U.S. dollar following the release of the news.

Tom Fairless of the Wall Street Journal, puts this issue right up front in his discussion of the move by the ECB.

"The ECB is now much more worried about supporting growth than snuffing out inflation, making cuts at consecutive meetings for just the first time since 2011."

"Europe faces a different set of problems from the U.S., where growth is strong."

Additional reductions in the policy rate are expected in the coming months.

Now, looking at the condition in Great Britain. Ed Frankl tells us in the Wall Street Journal:

"Inflation in the U.K. fell to below the Bank of England's 2% target in September for the first time in more than three years, boosting the chance of an interest-rate cut in November."

The British pound has dropped since this information on the inflation rate became available.

The market senses that in the case of Great Britain, there will also be more than one cut coming in the future.

Mr. Fairless states that Christine Lagarde, the president of the ECB, is cautioning investors to be patient, as decisions depend on the latest statistics.

"The ECB said it would continue to determine interest rates meeting by meeting based on incoming economic data, indicating a lack of certainty about exactly how bad things are."

"It wants to wait and see before committing to further cuts, though markets expect the ECB to lower interest rates again at its next meeting in December, to 3%."

But, further rate cuts are certainly under consideration.

So, this is the world that the Federal Reserve will be facing, moving into next year.

It is a world where the United States' economic growth "is strong," and where Europe faces "a different set of problems."

The question in the United States will be about how fast the Federal Reserve needs to lower its policy rate of interest.

If the U.S. economy is growing strongly, should the Federal Reserve cut its policy rate of interest further?

Inflation seems to be lessening as the Federal Reserve would like, but the Fed's target price index is still above the Fed's goal of 2%.

But, this sets up the dilemma.

If the ECB and the BOE are cutting their policy rates of interest (and there might be other central banks also cutting their policy rate of interest...like in Canada), the value of the U.S. dollar is going to continue to climb.

Here is a look at a "broad" index of the value of the U.S. dollar.

Here is a look at the value of the U.S. dollar against the Euro.

It is clear that Europe...and other parts of the world...face "a different set of problems" than the United States.

So, the United States must really determine what is important to it, and what it must really concentrate on.

The U.S. economy is growing at an acceptable pace.

Unemployment is close to the low levels it experienced 60 years ago.

And, inflation is closing in on the Fed's target rate.

The Federal Reserve does not need to further lower its policy rate of interest to "get the economy going" again. Concerning its macroeconomic policy stance, the Federal Reserve is in a very good position.

In fact, right at this particular time, the Federal Reserve is still in a policy stance of quantitative tightening, not because of the current performance of the macroeconomy, but because of all the liquidity that the Federal Reserve injected into the economy during the period of time when the COVID-19 pandemic was rapidly spreading in the United States...and the world.

The question, therefore, become, what should the U.S. policymakers be worried about?

Karl Rove, in his weekly column in the Wall Street Journal, argues that there are three challenges that the U.S. government is going to have to face.

The first challenge is the growing national debt. The current national debt totals $35.7 trillion.

The Congressional Budget Office is picturing that the debt will reach nearly $50.7 trillion by the end of 2034.

The second challenge is the projected bankruptcy of the Social Security Trust Fund. Again, the bust here is projected to come in the middle of the 2030s.

And, third, there is the fear that the U.S. is failing to keep up with the rest of the world in its national defense strategy.

Mr. Rove suggests that "these challenges won't go away...."

And, these looming problems will not be solved by the Federal Reserve pushing its policy interest rate lower and lower and lower.

The United States is leading the world in terms of its economic performance.

The United States needs to continue leading the world in terms of its economic performance.

However, the United States still has major concerns, as suggested by Mr. Rove, that need attention...and, these challenges won't go away.

The United States needs to move from its current position of strength and build programs that will address the issues suggested by Mr. Rove.

The dilemma is to determine how it is going to move ahead and resolve these other issues and continue to support a "strong" economy.

A strong U.S. dollar must be a part of the narrative!

data.title: Amicus: Teva Settlement Clears Revenue Path Forward For Galafold
data.description:
Amicus Therapeutics (NASDAQ:FOLD) now has a lot of breathing room with respect to its approved drug Galafold [migalastat] for the treatment of patients with Fabry disease. That's because it was just announced that it has settled its litigation against Teva Pharmaceutical (TEVA) with respect to a generic version of Galafold. Having said that, the agreement was made so that Teva would not be able to sell a generic version of this drug for the treatment of patients with Fabry Disease until at least January 30th of 2037. This opens the door for Amicus to at least generate as much revenue as possible until that date. Besides the company having already received this drug for regulatory approval, it has done so with another one in its pipeline.

This would be with respect to Pombiliti [cipaglucosidase alfa-atga] + Opfolda [miglustat] having been approved for the treatment of adults with late-onset Pompe Disease. The truth is that there is huge potential with this program as well, and that is because of expansion opportunities that have either happened already or are going to happen soon. For example, obtain several other regulatory approvals of this two-component therapy for these adults with late-onset Pompe Disease. Along with the ability to expand the label to eventually target children with late-onset Pompe Disease [LOPD] and infantile-onset Pompe Disease [IOPD]. With the Teva patent litigation suit out of the way, plus the ability for it to expand the use of Pombiliti + Opfolda for Pompe Disease, I believe that investors could benefit with any potential gains made here.

Teva Litigation Suit Ending Means A Clearer Path For Revenue Generation

As I stated above, Amicus was able to end its litigation against Teva Pharmaceutical for a generic version of Galafold for the treatment of patients with Fabry Disease. With this lysosomal storage disorder, the problem is that these patients don't have the proper alpha-galactosidase A [alpha-GAL]. What is wrong with this? The problem is that without this particular enzyme, the body can't break down fats in blood vessels and tissues of patients. Having said that, it can affect a multitude of parts of the body, such as the following:

  • Skin
  • Brain
  • Heart
  • Kidneys
  • Many other parts of the body

Thus, the types of symptoms such patients can have varies depending upon what is affected. For instance, if fat builds up on tissue of the heart, then it is possible that a person with Fabry Disease could suffer from a heart attack or stroke. Some other types of symptoms that these patients with Fabry Disease could experience are as follows:

  • Hearing loss
  • Rashes on the skin
  • Episodic pain in hand and feet
  • Issues With the gastrointestinal tract

The global Fabry Disease treatment market size is expected to reach $4.54 billion by 2033. This is a good market opportunity for sure, and the hope is that Amicus can continue to capitalize on this market. Sales of Galafold in Q2 of 2024 came in at $110.8 million, which is a year-over-year increase of 17%. With this continued strong growth of sales of this drug for the treatment of patients with Fabry Disease, it had raised its revenue growth guidance for 2024 to be between 14% to 18%.

The problem or overhang with Galafold is that Teva was gearing to launch a generic version of this drug for the treatment of this patient population. If a generic version were to be approved and marketed, then it would likely greatly impact revenue growth for Amicus going forward. Thus, it had filed a patent litigation case against this big pharmaceutical company. Such patent litigations can take years to conclude; thus it would have cost money and time. However, there was a positive development today, which is that Amicus announced a settlement of Galafold patent litigation with Teva. What happened exactly? Well, it was noted that Amicus would drop the patent litigation by providing Teva a license to market Galafold in the United States beginning on January 30th of 2037. The thing is that this might not even be all guaranteed, and the reason why is because Teva would still have to receive FDA approval of its generic version of Galafold for the treatment of patients with Fabry Disease.

Pompe Disease Program Continues With Expansion Opportunities

The thing about Amicus Therapeutics is that it is not purely relying on obtaining sales for its Fabry Disease drug Galafold. It has also been able to receive regulatory approvals for its two-component therapy Pombiliti + Opfolda for the treatment of adults with late-onset Pompe Disease. This disorder is classified as a condition in which a complex sugar, known as glycogen, builds up in the lysosomes of the body's cells. This actually is another lysosomal storage disorder that causes muscle weakness and wasting. The reason why this disease occurs is because of a mutation in the GAA gene, which is very bad considering that it is responsible for producing the alpha-glucosidase enzyme. This particular enzyme is responsible for breaking down glycogen into glucose [simple sugar]. Without this event happening, it leads to build up in organs and tissues of the body. In turn, it leads to the many symptoms that these patients can experience. Such symptoms that these patients with Pompe Disease experience are as follows:

  • Severe muscle weakness
  • Heart defect
  • Poor growth pattern
  • Respiratory problems
  • Ineffective weight gain
  • Enlarged liver in the body

The global Pompe Disease treatment market size is projected to grow to $1.53 billion by 2032. This is a very good market opportunity as well, on top of Galafold being approved to target patients with Fabry Disease.

The thing is that Pombiliti + Opfolda were approved by the FDA for the treatment of adults with late-onset Pompe Disease weighing ≥40 kg and who are not improving on their current enzyme replacement therapy [ERT]. Sales are actually coming in strong for this drug combination. In Q2 of 2024, it was noted that sales for these drugs were $15.9 million, which was a 44% increase from Q1 of 2024. It has already been approved in several territories for these Pompe Disease patients such as: Germany, United States, Austria, Spain and United Kingdom. However, it is quite possible that there might be additional expansion opportunities. The first possible opportunity would be in terms of expanding approval of these drugs towards even more territories. There are already multiple regulatory submissions expected during this year along. Along, with the fact that there are >10 reimbursement dossiers as well. Plus, you have to consider that it is not pining all of its hopes of only being able to generate revenues only in targeting adults with late-onset Pompe Disease. Instead, it is hoping to eventually expand the label of Pombiliti + Opfolda to target children with LOPD and infantile-onset Pompe Disease [IOPD]. These expansions towards these are patient populations are likely to end up resulting in increasing revenue to be made with these drugs. Sales are just coming in strong, and this is not just my opinion. It is based on the fact that its projection, of reaching between $62 - $67 million for full-year 2024 revenues of these drugs on a constant currency basis [CER], is likely to happen.

Financials

According to the 10-Q SEC Filing, Amicus Therapeutics had cash, cash equivalents and marketable securities of $260.1 million as of June 30th of 2024. The company is likely going to need to continue to sell stock and enact financial transactions continuously, as it is not yet profitable. This is based on the fact that it had to continuously sell different types of securities to raise cash. One such way that it could raise cash would be through a Sales Agreement made with The Goldman Sachs & Co. LLC to create an at-the-market equity program. With this agreement, the company could offer and sell up to $250 million worth of shares. As of June 30th, of 2024, it had an aggregate of $184.4 million worth of shares remaining available to be issued and sold under this ATM program. Total GAAP operating expenses for Q2 of 2024 was $100.4 million, which was a decrease of 4% from the year prior, with operating expenses of $104.2 million for Q2 of 2023.

Risks To Business

There are several risks that investors should be aware of before investing in Amicus Therapeutics. The first risk to consider would be in terms of the patent litigation suit being settled with Teva of Galafold for the treatment of patients with Fabry Disease. The reason why is because as soon as it hits January 30th of 2037 then the big pharma might be in a position to start selling its generic version of Galafold for Fabry Disease. Of course, that is barring that it first obtains FDA approval to actually sell the drug. The risk is that once Teva starts possibly marketing a generic version of this drug, then it would reduce the revenues that could be generated by Amicus for the treatment of these patients.

The second risk to consider would be in terms of Pombiliti + Opfolda sales for the treatment of patients with Pompe Disease. The company was able to produce $15.9 million in sales of these two-component drugs for the treatment of these patients in Q2 of 2024, which was an increase of 44% from Q1 of 2024. The risk here is that there is no guarantee that sales in the next quarter will turn out to be good, nor that such extensive double-digit growth will be achieved.

The third and final risk to consider would be in terms of the company being able to achieve several other regulatory approvals of Pombiliti + Opfolda for the treatment of patients with Pompe Disease. The risk here is that even though it was able to achieve marketing approvals for several territories as noted above, there is no assurance that it will be able to do so for the other expected territories. In addition, the goal is to be in a position to expand to target children with late-onset Pompe Disease [LOPD] and infantile-onset Pompe Disease [IOPD]. There is no guarantee that the studies being done for these other subpopulations will turn out to be positive, nor that it will be in a position to file for regulatory approval for them in one or any other territory.

Conclusion

Amicus Therapeutics has done well to clear a pathway in terms of being able to sell Galafold for the treatment of patients with Fabry Disease. It had the overhang of Teva being able to bring about a generic version of this drug targeting these patients. Such a generic version would have taken a lot of market share from the company. Thus, the best thing that could be done to end the patent litigation suit was to offer Teva a license to be able to sell its generic version on or after January 30th of 2037.

It had also made progress in being able to continue to grow revenues of Pombiliti + Opfolda for the treatment of patients with Pompe Disease. This was just observed with the release of the most recent earnings, where it was noted that revenues for these drugs in Q2 of 2024 were $15.9 million. This ended up being a 44% increase in revenues from Q1 of 2024. It was able to expand the use of these two-component therapies for Pompe Disease in Switzerland on July 4th of 2024. It has the ability to obtain additional regulatory approvals in other territories, along with the possibility of being able to expand the labels to eventually include children with LOPD and IOPD as well.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: ASML Earnings: Good Quarter, Derisking From China
data.description:
Investment Thesis

ASML Holding N.V. (NASDAQ:ASML) shares have fallen roughly 16% since my last analysis in September due to investors being spooked after the last earnings report.

The company's earnings announcement, which revealed weaker-than-expected bookings in their pipeline, made Wall Street analysts and investors anxious regarding ASML's growth pipeline, which is attributed to the Chinese market. ASML now projects 2025 net sales between €30 billion and €35 billion, down from previous estimates that suggested a range of €30 billion to €40 billion. While this guidance is more narrow, I think this is more of a conservative estimate than a reflection of weaker demand.

This is still technically in the reference range.

The biggest point of pressure in this sell-off appears to be driven by the increased regulatory headwinds faced by ASML exporting to China. Recent U.S. and Dutch export controls have increasingly limited ASML's ability to supply advanced semiconductor manufacturing equipment to Chinese customers. For now, this is a problem for their revenue pipeline.

As stated by the company's CFO, Roger Dassen, ASML expects that revenue from China will normalize next year to around 20% of total sales, down from 49% previously reported in 1H 2024.

Despite these immediate challenges, I actually remain bullish on ASML. The company is still going to benefit from the long-term growth in the semiconductor industry due to the high demand for AI use cases. While the current pipeline concerns are valid, they really miss ASML's potential for growth as the global demand for chip-making technology remains strong​.

ASML's technological leadership in extreme ultraviolet lithography (EUV) is still unmatched. I think the stock's recent decline could present a buying opportunity.

I know I took advantage of it.

Despite the dip in ASML's shares, I still think the innovative Dutch firm is a strong buy.

Why I'm Doing Follow-Up Coverage

To be honest, I did not expect to be writing on ASML so soon after my last analysis. However, the market's visceral reaction after the earnings report meant I wanted to provide my thoughts after the leaked earnings report surprised investors.

Despite the drop-off in shares earlier this week, ASML largely continues to be powering through the new export restrictions that are crimping their China business with strong Q3 revenue.

It was really their net bookings of €2.6 billion (compared to the €5.39 billion consensus estimate) that caused the sell-off.

At first glance, this raises a red flag and could suggest weaknesses in demand.

However, I really think these bookings should be considered with respect to where ASML is in their geographic revenue transition. The company has been moving away from their heavy reliance on the Chinese market, which likely was a big driver behind the decline in bookings quarter over quarter.

Keep in mind that bookings were actually up as well year-over-year.

As I touched on before, ASML's sales to China accounted for 49% of the company's revenue in the first half of the year. With expectations now set for this percentage to normalize to about 20% in the upcoming year, which reflects more of the geopolitical influences and regulatory changes rather than a decline in demand for their technology​.

This follow-up coverage of ASML is meant to focus on the company's repositioning away from China. The early earnings release caused market anxiety, but the fundamentals suggest that ASML's long-term outlook remains positive.

Earnings Review

After an accidental early earnings release on Tuesday, the Dutch company reported an EPS of $5.75, which beat analyst estimates by $0.46.

ASML's revenue for the quarter came in at $8.13 billion, which also surpassed the consensus estimate of $7.8 billion. This revenue beat, alongside the stronger-than-expected EPS, really shows that while there are challenges surrounding ASML's pipeline (as they move away from China)​, the company's fundamentals remain strong.

Since the earnings report, ASML has seen an uptick in downward revisions to forward EPS estimates because the market is concerned that this fall off in bookings is more long-term. The semiconductor equipment maker has seen its earnings estimates downgraded 13 times over the last 3 months. Based on the below chart of what the historical estimates have been, my guess is that most of this downward pressure on earnings estimates has shown up after the report this week. I think this is misguided.

The elephant in the room is the bookings for the quarter, so I want to address them.

China clearly appears to be a major driver of the bookings drop off, with the company facing these tighter export controls that happened to be announced this last quarter.

But if we look deeper, this is a necessary change for the business. As ASML's CFO mentioned as part of this earnings report, Chinese revenue currently amounts to 49% of sales from the first half of this year. But by next year? This is going to drop to just 20%, meaning the company will significantly de-risk their business from China.

At the same time, revenue is still expected to grow 15.21% next year.

Taking a step back, the company's current quarter was a transformative one. They have started to shift bookings (and sales) from China, and are bringing their exposure to the country down to just 20% of sales. At the same time, revenue is still set to grow next year. So by the end of 2025, the company will be far less exposed to China, booking more revenue and the overall story will be less risky. These are all bullish signs.

This was a big part of the research I put out on the company back in June. I was (and am) a big believer that the company can outgrow their China exposure. This is turning out to be true.

To help smooth out this transition, the company still has an order backlog of over €36 billion, which provides an excellent buffer against the current volatility in bookings.

Keep in mind that these are still very profitable orders. ASML CFO Roger Dassen stated on the earnings call when asked about their Q4 guidance:

We expect Q4 total net sales to be between EUR8.8 billion and EUR9.2 billion. We expect our Q4 installed base management sales to be around EUR1.9 billion. Gross margin for Q4 is expected to be between 49% and 50% -Q3 Call.

Valuation

ASML currently trades at a non-GAAP forward P/E of 34.09, which is well above the sector median of 24.34.

Their P/E premium of 40.05% tells me that despite this quarter, the market still believes in the company's leadership in the key EUV equipment market (and also thinks this market will help the Dutch company out earn peers over the coming years).

On the surface, while this P/E ratio might seem elevated, this is actually 14.99% below ASML's five-year average forward P/E of 40.10. What the market is saying here with this relative discount to their 5-year average is that ASML is still going to grow its earnings faster than its peers going forward. However, it will be at a rate that is lower than its 5-year trailing average. I think this assumption is incorrect.

The future for new EUV machines is bright even without China. Companies like Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited (TSM), who is a key customer that just happened to also report this week, provided a strong outlook for their earnings. A strong TSMC means ASML gets to benefit from strong order flow. I am optimistic.

With this, I think shares should trade back at their 5-year average forward P/E. This would represent a 17.62% upside.

Risks

With China being de-risked, one of the other main risks identified by Wall Street analysts stems from their concern that the semiconductor fab market may be overbuilt in Western countries. I think this is a complete misnomer, and I am thankful that the TSMC CEO shut this notion down on their conference call this week with their strong earnings report.

TSMC is implying they are going to spend $30 billion in CapEx this year to help upgrade their fabs to support the immense demand for AI chips as companies build out their AI hyper-scalers. TSMC has only spent $6 billion/quarter on CapEx so far this year. This means they have an additional $12 billion to spend by year's end, according to analysts' estimates. Much of this CapEx spend will roll down to ASML.

On top of this, TSMC's CFO noted that CapEx spend will probably go up next year. This is highly bullish for ASML.

Bottom Line

While ASML's recent earnings report revealed a sequential decline in bookings, primarily due to geopolitical pressures impacting their business in China, the company is still on a strong growth trajectory over the long run. The Dutch firm is smart to cut their geographic revenue exposure from this market from the historical 50% revenue marker, down to about 20% of overall revenue moving forward.

ASML's fundamentals remain strong, with net sales exceeding expectations and gross margins holding steady at roughly 50%. The company's ability to grow fast in other markets supports my bullish outlook.

Although ASML's valuation may seem steep based on traditional price-to-earnings metrics, the underlying growth potential justifies the company's P/E moving higher back to its 5-year average, meaning shares have an upside from here. With this, I believe ASML shares are still a strong buy​. I firmly believe the recent sell-off presents a buying opportunity (one I took advantage of), as the market undervalues ASML's growth prospects.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Gold Price Smashes Through $2700/Oz - Further Gains Ahead?
data.description:
By Zain Vawda

Gold prices advanced further overnight, gaining acceptance above the $2700/oz as global rate cut bets intensified. The killing of Hamas Political Bureau leader and of the masterminds behind the October 7 attacks, Yahya Sinwar, had raised expectations of an escalation in the Middle East conflict, but the precious metal was already well on its way to fresh highs.

Currently, a mix of factors is fueling the gold rally. Despite the strengthening US dollar, gold prices continue to climb. Economic data from the UK and the ECB’s interest rate meeting have boosted expectations for rate cuts worldwide, enhancing gold’s attractiveness. Lower global interest rates reduce the opportunity cost of holding this non-yielding precious metal and could keep the rally moving forward.

A bullish take from the London Bullion Market Association, which conducted a poll recently, further adds credence to the idea that gold prices may not be done just yet. The poll was to predict the price of gold in 12-months time with the association seeing prices at $2941/oz.

The US election is nearing as well, and uncertainty continues around the next US President. This could be another reason the appeal of safe haven continues to grow.

Technical Analysis Gold (XAU/USD)

From a technical analysis standpoint, gold has been difficult to analyze with the lack of price action.

Gold bears may have been hoping for some headwinds from US data, but that has not materialized as housing data disappointed. This has led to some USD weakness, which in theory should aid gold prices.

The concern for bulls lies in the fact that the RSI is now in overbought territory on the four-hour, daily, and weekly charts. That coupled with the potential for profit-taking before the end of the day leaves me slightly concerned. However, the threat of a retaliatory strike by Israel on Iran has strengthened as Israeli officials commented today a strike is imminent. This is something that could limit downside ahead of the weekend and into next week as well.

Immediate support rests at 2700 before the 2685 and 2673 handles come into focus.

Conversely, looking at the upside and immediate resistance rests at today’s high print around 2717 before 2725 and 2750 come into focus.

Gold (XAU/USD) Four-Hour (H4) Chart, October 18, 2024

Support

  • 2700
  • 2685
  • 2673

Resistance

  • 2717
  • 2725
  • 2750

Original Post

data.title: The Ford Motor Absurdity: Yield Exceeds P/E
data.description:
F stock: 7% yield meets 6x P/E

I last wrote on the Ford Motor Company (NYSE:F) back in April 2024. That article was entitled “Ford Motor: P/E Is A Poor Indicator Of Cyclical Low”. As you can already guess from the title, the article analyzed of the status of its business cycle. Quote:

Ford is currently trading at a low P/E ratio of 6.5x FWD EPS. The single-digit P/E certainly does not hurt a value proposition. But the main reasons for my bull considerations are the other signs that indicate F is near or in the expansion phase of a new cycle.

Since that writing, there have been several key developments surrounding the stock. On the top of my list is its recent earnings report (for its FY Q2 2024). The results were weaker than expected. Both the top line and bottom line were well below consensus expectations. The key issues in my view include the uptick in its increased warranty costs and the lingering quality issues of some of its cars. I anticipate further costs and investments are required to address these issues and raise the caliber of its vehicles.

As such, the market has good reasons to be concerned. However, the current market sentiment is so extreme that it has created a very absurd situation in my view. The stock is not providing an FWD dividend yield of 7.05% as you can see from the screenshot below, which exceeds its FWD P/E of 5.9x (that is, if you only consider the numerical values and ignore the percentage sign).

With this background, in the rest of this article, I will argue that the market has overacted and this absurdity has created a very asymmetric return/risk curve.

F stock: Market’s EPS growth outlook

As another reflection of the market’s extreme sentiment on the stock, the following chart shows the consensus estimates for F stock in the next five years.

The EPS projections indicate a very gloomy outlook for the company's earnings growth. In the near term, F is expected to see a decline of -7% year-over-year growth rate for the current FY 2024. Its earnings are then projected to rebound slightly (1.58% and 0.93% for FY 2025 and 2026, respectively) after that. Overall, the market expects its earnings to grow from $1.87 per share in 2024 to $1.92 per share in 5 years, translating into an annual growth rate of essentially 0% in CAGR terms.

Next, I will argue why such a projection is too pessimistic.

F stock: my growth projection

My method for estimating the organic growth rate (i.e., not considering bolt-on acquisitions or divestitures) is based on the return on capital employed (“ROCE”) and the reinvestment rate (“RR”). The method has been detailed in my other articles and here I will only quote the end results below:

The ROCE for F has been around 39.3% on average in recent years as seen in the chart below. Its RR is about 15% on average. With these inputs, F’s organic growth rate would be ~5.9% (39.3% ROCE x 15% RR = 5.9% growth rate). Note this number is the real growth rate without inflation. To obtain a notional growth rate, one would need to add an inflation escalator, which can further push the growth rate up.

I do see plenty of areas for F to invest its capital with robust returns and support my above growth projection. At the top of my list is its hybrid vehicles. Meanwhile, Ford is making advancements. Sales of hybrid vehicles jumped 34% in the last quarter. Going ahead, I expect the momentum to continue in the next 1~2 years as the adoption of fully electric vehicles has been slower than the market originally anticipated. The company has ongoing projects to ramp up the production of hybrids and is very well-positioned to capitalize on this front.

The next most promising growth area on my list involves its Ford Pro segment. (This segment serves its commercial customers.) This segment has also been reporting strong growth lately thanks to the strong demand for its Super Duty trucks. In response to such demand, the company recently announced plans to build a third assembly plant in Ontario. When the plan is fully operational, I anticipate it to add production of up to 100k units of its popular trucks, adding a considerable boost to its earnings.

Other risks and final thoughts

Since I started with a contrast of its P/E and yield, let me revisit the contrast before closing. As another way to illustrate how absurd the current price-value mismatch is, let’s consider the so-called PEGY ratio. I consider the PEGY ratio to be a more insightful metric for dividend stocks than the usual PEG ratio (P/E growth) because of the following reasons Peter Lynch promoted:

For dividend stocks, Lynch uses a revised version of the PEG ratio - the PEGY ratio, which is defined as the P/E ratio divided by the sum of the earnings growth rate and dividend yield. The idea behind the PEGY is very simple and effective (most effective ideas are simple). If a stock pays out a large part of its earnings as dividends, then investors do not need a high growth rate to enjoy healthy returns. And vice versa. And similar to the PEG ratio, his preference is a PEGY ratio of 1x or below.

Under F’s current conditions, with an FWD P/E of 5.9x and a dividend yield of 7%+, the PEGY ratio is only 0.85x even if you assume zero growth as the consensus projections imply. If you plug in my projected growth rate of 5.9%, the PEGY ratio would only be 0.46x, less than half of the 1x threshold.

To conclude, the market of course has valid reasons to have a negative sentiment on F. Besides the intense competition in the automobile sector (that many analysts have thoroughly discussed), F is also experiencing some more immediate and particular issues. As aforementioned, its ongoing warranty costs and quality issues are at the top of my list. Looking further ahead, F needs to strike a good balance between maintaining quality while reducing manufacturing costs and complexity. It is a crucial and significant challenge as it ramps up its production and develops new models. These issues definitely warrant a discounted valuation to compensate for the uncertainties. My point is that the discount has gone too far to the degree of being absurd. My investing experience has taught me that market absurdity usually creates outsized returns.

data.title: Netflix: Q3 Shows No One Can Stop This Streaming Giant
data.description:
Investment Thesis

The last time I wrote about Netflix (NASDAQ: NASDAQ:NFLX) in July 2024, I analysed the company’s second quarter earnings report and highlighted how the company’s hybrid sports strategy should allow it to stay ahead of its competitors. I had a BUY rating on the stock.

Since my article was published, the stock has jumped 17.8%, handily outperforming the S&P 500, which only gained 5.2% during the same period.

In this article, I analyse the company’s Q3 earnings report and dissect the major developments, especially the progress of its ads business.

Third Quarter Highlights

Netflix had an outstanding quarter in my opinion with the streaming giant beating on top- and bottom-line estimates once again. Q3 revenues came in at $9.82 billion, up 15% y/y and beating analyst estimates by $57.65 million. Diluted EPS of $5.40 was up 44.8% y/y and comfortably beat estimates by $0.27. While global streaming paid memberships grew 14.4% y/y for the quarter, net subscriber additions saw a considerable fall, down 42% y/y. The third quarter of last year was when the company’s password-sharing crackdown took shape, which explains the y/y decline. However, the company’s focus on profitability was clear, as operating margins came in at 29.6%, much stronger than the 22.4% figure it attained during the same period last year. Operating margins were also ahead of the company’s own guidance of 28.1%. Free cash flows also showed considerable growth, coming in at $2.19 billion, an impressive y/y growth of 15.9%.

The company once again delivered strong guidance. Q4 revenues are projected to come in at $10.13 billion, a y/y growth of 14.7%. Operating margins for the fourth quarter are expected to come in at 21.6%, which would be a significant jump from the 16.9% it generated in Q4 of last year. Diluted EPS is expected to come in at $4.23, which would translate to a massive y/y growth of 100.5%. The company also expects paid net additions to be higher in the fourth quarter, on a sequential basis, due to “normal seasonality and a strong content slate.” If these Q4 estimates come true, then it would imply that FY24 revenues would grow 15% y/y (high-end of company’s full-year guidance) and the higher-than-expected revenues have led the management to project FY24 operating margins of 27% (vs. previous guidance of 26%). Finally, management expects FY25 revenues to come in between $43 and $44 billion (y/y growth of 11-13%) and FY25 operating margins to be at 28%.

Netflix’s Content Slate Demonstrates How It Has Won the Streaming Wars

The major highlight of the company’s third quarter, to me, was how the company’s content slate has managed to not only drive subscriber growth, especially in the ads-tier, but also enabled the company to deliver strong subscriber engagement. Subscriber engagement, a metric that NFLX uses to demonstrate customer retention, for Q3, came in at about two hours per member per day. This could be attributed to the company's strong content slate, which included the likes of Perfect Couple, Monsters: The Lyle and Erik Menendez Story, and the latest seasons of Emily in Paris and Cobra Kai. It not only allowed the company to deliver strong revenue and margin growth, but also enabled it to build a strong membership base for its ads business, as evidenced by the 35% q/q jump in ads membership. The jump in ads membership has been so strong that it has exceeded the company’s ability to monetize its ad inventory.

Looking ahead, the company, in the fourth quarter, is set to launch one of the strongest content slates it has ever released in recent times in my opinion. This includes the likes of Season 2 of Squid Games, Season 4 of Outer Banks, and Season 7 of Love is Blind. At the same time, the company is also set to launch two of its most ambitious shows from Latin America:a biopic on legendary F1 driver Ayrton Senna, and 100 Years of Solitude, a show based on the iconic novel of the same name by legendary author Gabriel Garcia Marquez. Then, there is the much talked about Mike Tyson vs Jake Paul boxing match as well as 2 massive NFL matches on Christmas day, as the company ramps up its live sports programming ahead of the streaming debut of WWE Raw, which kicks off in January 2025. Finally, 2025 will also see the return of Stranger Things and Happy Gilmore along with new shows from Shonda Rhimes and Ryan Murphy.

In my opinion, NFLX has already won the streaming wars, as evidenced by its consistent revenue and profitability growth. The company even overtook a legacy Hollywood studio in the US for the first time, in Q3, with respect to demand for original programming. All of this can be attributed to its ability to consistently deliver high-quality content, which keeps users engaged. Q3 was no different. Moreover, for Q4, the company has already given some strong guidance, as mentioned in the previous section, which once again can be attributed to its content. The strong content slate has even allowed the company to increase prices in multiple regions, with Italy and Spain being the latest countries to join the list. As Netflix is set to finish the year on a high, the latest quarter proved why no competitor can come close to it.

Ads Business Shows Promise but Uncertainty Remains

When a company’s management comes out and says that it is unable to keep up with demand, it usually means positive news. While the same can be said about NFLX’s ads business, the uncertainty surrounding this business remains, which should leave investors uneasy, especially since I see its ads business as the next big catalyst for its growth. The positive takeaway, with respect to the ads business, during the third quarter, was the tremendous progress made by the company in growing its ads-tier membership. For the third quarter, as mentioned earlier, membership for its ads-tier grew 35% q/q, building on the 34% sequential growth seen in the second quarter. The ads-tier accounted for over 50% of sign-ups in countries where the company has introduced this plan. In terms of engagement, the company found that members in this tier were engaging with similar content as the non-ads plan members. Both sets also had similar views. All of these highlights are positive and banishes the questions surrounding cannibalisation of the non-ads plan and whether the ads plan would negatively impact both margins and overall profitability.

Having said that, there are still question marks surrounding the company’s ability to monetize its growing ads inventory. For a while now, management has mentioned that they are in the early stages of the ads business and that a lot of work remains to be done. Q3 was no different as the management, during the earnings call, reiterated this message. The company remains on track to launch its own ad server in Canada during Q4 before expanding it to the rest of the ads market in 2025. However, management reiterated during the earnings call that the ads business will not be making any meaningful contribution to its top- and bottom-lines in FY25.

The last time I wrote about NFLX, I did mention that investors shouldn’t judge its ads business harshly as it is making progress with each passing quarter. While I maintain that stance, I would have still preferred to see some more commentary on the progress made in this space. Instead, the message was largely the same as Q2. While there is no reason to panic given that the company’s content slate is too strong that it should help the company to grow even in FY25, the uncertainty surrounding monetization of its ads business is an area that I can’t ignore. If the same message is maintained in the fourth quarter as well, then it would be extremely concerning.

Valuation

Forward P/E Multiple Approach

Price Target

$782.00

Projected Forward P/E Multiple

33.9x

Projected FY25 EPS

$23.06

Source: LSEG Data (formerly Refinitiv), Author’s Projections, and Company’s Q3FY24 Letter to Shareholders

As mentioned earlier, management expects FY25 revenues to come in between $43 and $44 billion. Given the company’s FY25 content slate, which includes the likes of Squid Games Season 2, Stranger Things series finale, a new season of Wednesday, a new Knives Out film as well as the arrival of WWE Raw, I expect the company to have a strong year overall. FY24 revenues are set to match the company’s high-end of guidance, which was also my estimate, and given that the content slate is even more attractive for FY25 in my opinion, I don’t see any reason why the company can’t repeat the feat again in FY25. As such, I have assumed FY25 revenues to come in at $44 billion, the high-end of the company’s guidance.

Management now expects FY25 operating margins to come in at 28%, expanding by a 100 bps from FY24. This is a reasonable estimate in my opinion, given that the company has been managing its expenses in a disciplined manner, an argument I raised in my previous article on the company as well. Therefore, I have assumed FY25 operating margins of 28% for my calculations, which translates to $12.32 billion.

I have assumed FY25 non-operating expenses for the company to be $708 million, which would be approximately the average net non-operating expenses in the last five years. I have left my effective tax rate assumption to be the same as last time, which is 13.3%. Taken together, this would result in total FY25 net income of $10.1 billion. According to the company’s latest income statement (unaudited), the weighted average shares of common stock used to calculate the diluted EPS was 437.9 million. Using this figure, FY25 diluted EPS is projected to come in at $23.06, slightly lower than my previous estimate of $24.23.

The company, according to LSEG Data, currently trades at a forward P/E of 30.8x, which is close to its historical 2-year forward P/E multiple of 29.7x. Given that the company is dominating the streaming landscape, and given the structural tailwinds such as a stronger content slate in FY25, struggling competition, and the untapped potential of its advertising business, I believe it needs to trade at its 5-year historical median multiple of 33.9x. As such, I have used this multiple for my calculations.

A forward P/E of 33.9x and a projected EPS of $23.06 results in a price target of $782, which is higher than my previous target of $734, and which suggests a 14% upside from Thursday’s close. I am maintaining my BUY rating on the stock.

Risk Factors

The main risk, in addition to the uncertainty surrounding the company’s ads business, to my bull-case, involves the potential issues surrounding live streaming, a point that I raised in my last two articles on the company. While the company has largely avoided any major issues for its live programming since the Love is Blind fiasco, the stakes are much higher for the company when it comes to the two NFL games on Christmas Day and WWE. Any issues would have a significant negative impact on both the future revenues and profitability.

Concluding Thoughts

NFLX had yet another outstanding quarter in my opinion, beating on both the top- and bottom-lines once again. Management also offered strong guidance, both for the fourth quarter, as well as for FY25.

The latest quarter perfectly demonstrated the company’s ability to keep churning out high-quality content on a regular basis. With some of its biggest hits returning with new seasons in the fourth quarter as well as in FY25, and with the arrival of both NFL and WWE in its live programming category, NFLX has all but won the streaming wars in my opinion.

If there is an area of concern, it would be the uncertainty surrounding the company’s ability to monetize the ads inventory. Despite seeing strong membership growth in its ads tier, the company, during the latest quarter, did not offer any new insights into its plans to monetize its ads business. Management merely stated that the business would take time to grow, and it would have no meaningful impact on the company’s revenues and profits in FY25.

From a valuation perspective, there is still upside from current levels. And with a strong grade of A- on Seeking Alpha’s Momentum scale, I won’t be surprised if the stock blows past even my updated price target. This is Netflix’s world, and we are all just living in it. Q3 was a testament to that.
